fields.name: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
fields.definition: A common but serious mood disorder characterized by persistently depressed mood or loss of interest or pleasure in activities, lasting at least two weeks and causing significant functional impairment. To meet diagnostic criteria, a person must experience five or more of the specified symptoms including depressed mood and/or loss of interest, along with changes in sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, self-worth, or thoughts of death or suicide. MDD is not the same as normal sadness — it is a medical condition with neurobiological underpinnings.
fields.example: A person with MDD feels persistently hopeless, loses interest in hobbies they previously enjoyed, sleeps 12 hours a day, and cannot concentrate at work — symptoms lasting more than 3 weeks.

fields.name: Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD)
fields.definition: A chronic form of depression (formerly called dysthymia) in which depressed mood persists for at least 2 years in adults (1 year in children). Symptoms may be less severe than in a major depressive episode but are long-lasting and interfere with functioning. People with PDD may experience one or more major depressive episodes on top of their baseline chronic low mood — a pattern sometimes called 'double depression.' Because symptoms are chronic and may be normalized, PDD is often underrecognized.
fields.example: A person who has felt 'down most of the time' for 3 years, struggles with low energy and poor self-esteem, but has not had a severe enough episode to clearly identify as major depression.

fields.name: Postpartum Depression
fields.definition: A serious form of depression occurring in parents (most commonly birthing parents) typically within the first year after delivery. It is more severe and longer-lasting than the 'baby blues' (mild mood changes in the first 2 weeks postpartum) and involves full depressive symptoms including persistent sadness, difficulty bonding with the baby, withdrawal from family and friends, and in severe cases, thoughts of harming oneself or the infant. Contributing factors include the dramatic hormonal shifts after childbirth, sleep deprivation, and social stressors.
fields.example: A new parent feels persistently numb and disconnected from their newborn three months after delivery, struggles with severe guilt, and stops eating — requiring clinical intervention.

fields.name: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)
fields.definition: A type of depression with a seasonal pattern, most commonly beginning in fall or winter and remitting in spring. SAD is related to reduced sunlight exposure affecting circadian rhythms and serotonin and melatonin levels. Symptoms include the standard MDD criteria plus seasonal-specific features: oversleeping, increased appetite and carbohydrate craving, weight gain, and a heavy, leaden feeling in the limbs. Less commonly, a summer pattern of SAD exists. First-line treatments include light therapy (bright light exposure in the morning), antidepressants, and CBT adapted for SAD.
fields.example: Each November through February, a person gains 10 pounds, sleeps 10 hours a night, and cannot motivate for work — symptoms resolving spontaneously by April.

fields.title: Symptoms of Major Depressive Disorder (DSM-5 Criteria)
fields.body: To be diagnosed with MDD, a person must experience five or more of the following symptoms during the same 2-week period, with at least one being either depressed mood or loss of interest/pleasure: (1) Depressed mood most of the day, nearly every day; (2) Markedly diminished interest or pleasure in almost all activities; (3) Significant weight loss or gain, or change in appetite; (4) Insomnia or hypersomnia; (5) Psychomotor agitation or slowing observable by others; (6) Fatigue or loss of energy; (7) Feelings of worthlessness or excessive inappropriate guilt; (8) Difficulty thinking, concentrating, or making decisions; (9) Recurrent thoughts of death, suicidal ideation, or a suicide attempt. Symptoms must cause significant distress or impairment and must not be attributable to substance use or another medical condition.

The monoamine hypothesis proposes that depression is associated with reduced activity of monoamine neurotransmitters — particularly serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine — in the brain. Most antidepressants work by increasing the availability of one or more of these neurotransmitters at the synapse. SSRIs (selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors) block the reuptake pump for serotonin, raising serotonin levels in the synapse. SNRIs block reuptake of both serotonin and norepinephrine. TCAs (tricyclics) and MAOIs act through different but related mechanisms. Antidepressants typically require 4–8 weeks to produce meaningful symptom improvement, reflecting downstream changes in receptor sensitivity and neuroplasticity rather than just immediate neurotransmitter changes.
fields.name: How Antidepressants Work
fields.example: A person starts fluoxetine (an SSRI). After 6 weeks, serotonin signaling has normalized and they notice improved mood, energy, and concentration.

fields.name: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (SSRIs)
fields.definition: The most commonly prescribed class of antidepressant medications. SSRIs selectively block the reuptake of serotonin into the presynaptic neuron, increasing serotonin availability in the synapse. They are used as first-line treatment for MDD and several other conditions (anxiety disorders, OCD, PTSD). Common SSRIs include fluoxetine (Prozac), sertraline (Zoloft), escitalopram (Lexapro), paroxetine (Paxil), and citalopram (Celexa). They generally have a more tolerable side-effect profile than older antidepressant classes.
fields.example: A physician prescribes sertraline as the first medication trial for a patient with moderate MDD due to its established efficacy and tolerability.

fields.body: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for depression begins by helping the person identify automatic negative thoughts — distorted interpretations that reinforce depressed mood, such as catastrophizing, overgeneralization, and all-or-nothing thinking. The therapist uses cognitive restructuring to examine the evidence for these thoughts and replace them with more balanced perspectives. Behavioral activation is the second major component: depression causes withdrawal and inactivity, which deepens depression. Scheduling and gradually increasing engagement in meaningful or pleasant activities breaks this cycle. CBT for depression typically involves 12–20 structured sessions and has comparable efficacy to antidepressants for mild-to-moderate depression, with lower relapse rates after treatment ends.
fields.name: CBT for Depression
fields.example: A person with depression schedules a 10-minute walk daily as a behavioral activation task, using it as a foundation for building other activities as mood gradually improves.

fields.question: How many US adults experience a major depressive episode each year?
fields.answer: An estimated 21.0 million US adults (8.3% of all US adults) had at least one major depressive episode in 2021. Prevalence was higher among women (10.3%) than men (6.2%). Among adolescents aged 12–17, approximately 20.1% (5.0 million) had at least one major depressive episode — making depression particularly prevalent in that age group.

fields.question: What is the key difference between clinical depression and normal sadness?
fields.answer: Normal sadness is a proportionate emotional response to difficult life events (grief, disappointment) and typically resolves as circumstances change. Clinical depression (MDD) is distinguished by its duration (at least 2 weeks), its severity (multiple symptoms affecting multiple aspects of life), its functional impact (inability to work, care for oneself, or maintain relationships), and its persistence regardless of external circumstances. Depression is also neurobiological — it involves measurable changes in brain chemistry and structure, not just a reaction to events.

fields.question: How long must symptoms persist for a Major Depressive Disorder diagnosis?
fields.answer: Symptoms must be present for at least 2 weeks. During this period, the person must experience five or more specified symptoms, with at least one being either depressed mood or loss of interest or pleasure. Symptoms must cause significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other areas of functioning.

fields.name: Electroconvulsive Therapy (ECT)
fields.definition: A medical procedure in which brief electrical stimulation of the brain is applied under general anesthesia to produce a controlled seizure. ECT is one of the most effective treatments for severe, treatment-resistant depression and can produce rapid improvement. It is used when depression has not responded to multiple medication trials, when it is life-threatening (e.g., person has stopped eating or is at imminent suicide risk), or when rapid response is needed. Modern ECT is safe and performed under anesthesia; the electrical impulses are applied to specific brain regions using optimized techniques that minimize cognitive side effects.
fields.example: A person hospitalized for severe MDD who has not responded to three medication trials receives a course of ECT and experiences significant symptom relief within 2 weeks.

fields.question: How does depression relate to suicide risk?
fields.answer: Depression is a significant risk factor for suicidal thoughts and behaviors. Recurrent thoughts of death or suicidal ideation are among the DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for MDD. Effective treatment for depression reduces suicide risk. If you or someone you know is in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide and Crisis Lifeline by calling or texting 988. Brain stimulation therapies like ECT may be used as earlier interventions when depression is life-threatening.

fields.title: Depression and Physical Symptoms
fields.body: Depression is not only a mood disorder — it produces significant physical symptoms that can precede, accompany, or outlast the emotional symptoms. Common physical manifestations include: persistent fatigue regardless of sleep amount; unexplained aches and pains, including headaches and back pain; gastrointestinal problems (nausea, cramping, changes in bowel habits); changes in appetite and weight; sleep disturbances (insomnia or hypersomnia); slowed movement and speech observable to others (psychomotor retardation); and a heavy, weighted feeling in the body. These physical symptoms are neurobiologically real — not 'just in the person's head' — and often respond to the same treatments that address depressive mood.

fields.question: Is depression just sadness — can people choose to feel better or snap out of it?
fields.answer: No. NIMH states directly: "Everyone feels sad or low sometimes, but these feelings usually pass. Depression is different." Clinical depression requires symptoms lasting most of the day, nearly every day, for at least two weeks, severe enough to interfere with daily functioning including work, sleep, and relationships. NIMH frames depression as a medical condition — not a mood, a character flaw, or a choice. The emphasis on diagnosable criteria, neurobiological factors, and required professional treatment makes clear that depression cannot be resolved through attitude adjustment or force of will.
